segments.s324-g9: همان is a pointing determiner meaning 'that' or 'the same' placed before the noun to pick out a previously mentioned person; طوس is the proper name Tus. با + noun is a prepositional phrase 'with ...' and here کاویانی functions adjectivally with درفش to yield 'the Kayanian banner/standard'. Word order is determiner + name followed by a 'با' phrase giving accompaniment.
segments.s324-g10: This is a compact noun phrase. همه is a quantifier 'all/every', نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. زرینه modifies کفش ('gilded/golden shoes'). The predicate verb is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis) so the phrase reads as 'all the renowned ones (were) in gilded shoes.'
segments.s324-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گودرز is the subject (proper name). پیش تهمتن is a locative/relational phrase 'before/in the presence of Tahmatan' (پیش = 'in front of/before'). رسید is a past‑tense verb 'arrived/reached'. The clause has the order conjunction + subject + locative phrase + past verb.
segments.s324-g12: سرشکش is a noun 'a tear‑drop' as the clause's subject. ز (poetic variant of از) marks source 'from' and governs مژگان 'the eyelashes'. به here marks direction/target 'onto/towards' and governs رخ 'the face/cheek'. برچکید is a past/telic verb meaning 'fell/dripped forth'. The sequence is subject + source phrase + target phrase + verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s324-g13: سپاهی functions as the subject 'the army/forces'. همی is an archaic/fixed frequentive/progressive particle that here gives an ongoing past sense ('kept on / was repeatedly'); رفت is the past of 'go' meaning 'marched on'. رخساره زرد is a predicative phrase 'countenance/complexion (was) pale' — adjective after noun is normal in poetry.
segments.s324-g14: ز خسرو is an ablative/cause phrase 'because of / for Khosrow' (ز = از). همه دل 'every heart' (quantifier + noun) acts as subject of the following description. پر is the predicate adjective 'full (of)', and از introduces the contents 'of dāgh and dard' — داغ here means 'burning grief/anguish' and درد 'pain/sorrow'. The clause structure is cause phrase + subject + predicate + of‑phrase.
segments.s324-g15: بگفتند is the simple past 'they said/spoke'. با here marks the addressee in the sense 'to/with' (classical usage allows با + person = 'to someone'); زال و رستم are the names of the addressees. که introduces reported speech 'that', and شاه is the subject of the reported clause. Word order is reporting verb + indirect object phrase + complementizer + subject of reported speech.
segments.s324-g16: به گفتار ابلیس is a causal/instrumental phrase 'by the counsel/speech of Iblis' (به used here as 'by'/'through'). گم is an adjectival/predicative element 'lost/astray', and کرد is a light/casual verb 'made/caused' used with گم to yield 'caused to be lost' — together meaning '(he) lost the way' with راه 'way/path' as the object. Poetic word order places the object after the light verb.
segments.s324-g1: سر هفته را is a temporal phrase placed at the front: سر (literally 'head/top') + هفته = 'at the start of the week'. The particle را here topicalizes/emphasizes that time phrase. زال و رستم is the compound subject and به هم precedes the verb in the next segment to form the reciprocal expression 'to one another / each other'.
segments.s324-g2: رسیدند is past 3rd‑person plural of رسیدن; here combined with the preceding به هم it means 'they met' (lit. 'they reached each other'). بی‌کام functions as an adjective 'unfulfilled' modifying دل; پر ز غم is a predicative phrase 'full of sorrow' where پر means 'full (of)' and ز introduces the cause/source 'from/of sorrow'.
segments.s324-g3: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians'. آگهی یافته(ند) is a compound in which یافتند (past 'they found/learned') takes آگهی ('news/information') as its object: 'when the Iranians learned the news'.
segments.s324-g4: همه is 'all/everyone' (subject). داغ دل is a fixed‑sense collocation 'burning grief/aching heart' (داغ modifying دل). پیش here has a locative/forward sense ('before/forward, into the presence of') and with the verb بشتافتند ('they hurried') yields 'they rushed forward/to the place'. The verb بشتافتند is past plural 'they hurried'.
segments.s324-g5: چو 'when' introduces the clause. رستم پدید آمد is 'Rostam appeared' (پدید آمد = 'came into view/appeared', past). The conjunction و links that clause to the short descriptive tag زال زر, where زر is used elliptically to describe Zal as adorned/associated with gold (poetic apposition).
segments.s324-g6: همان is 'that/the same' pointing back; موبدان is the plural of موبد 'mōbed/priests'. فراوان here qualifies هنر and means 'abundant/plentiful', so the nominal phrase فراوان هنر = 'abundant skill' or 'much skill'. The verb is omitted by poetic ellipsis; the phrase as a whole describes that those same priests displayed ample skill.
segments.s324-g7: هر آن کس که بود is a common relative formula 'every one who was' (هر ... کس که بود = 'whoever was'). از introduces the source/lineage; نژاد is 'race/lineage' and زرسب is an epithet 'golden' (describing the lineage). The clause as a whole restricts to those of the golden stock.
segments.s324-g8: پذیره شدن is a verbal noun/infinitival phrase 'being received / reception'; را is the definite/object marker marking that phrase as the object/topic of the following verb. بیاراست is an older/poetic perfective 'adorned / prepared' (here 'had prepared/adorned'), and اسب is the direct object 'horse/steed' — together: 'they prepared a steed for the reception'.
segments.s324-g33: شما (you) is the subject in front (polite/plural). دل مدارید is a present indicative/continuous phrase: دل (heart) + مدارید (an older/poetic verbal form meaning to direct/turn one’s attention or feeling toward — here describing a repeated or habitual action by ‘you’). چندین به غم is a circumstantial phrase: چندین (many/often) modifies the following prepositional phrase به غم (toward/into grief), so the whole segment means ‘you often turn/settle your hearts toward sorrow.’
segments.s324-g34: که introduces a result/reason clause ('such that' / 'so that'); از غم is a simple from/of phrase ('from sorrow'); شود is the present subjunctive/impersonal form of شدن used here to mean 'becomes' (it yields a resulting state); جان is the patient/theme 'the soul' or 'life'; خرم دژم are two contrasting adjectives placed side by side for poetic effect ('khorram' = joyful, 'deżam' = sullen/gloomy). The clause therefore says that, as a result of sorrow, the soul takes on these alternating states.
segments.s324-g35: بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ('let us try' / 'we should strive'). و connects the two coordinated verbs. بسیار modifies پندش (much/many a piece of advice), and پندش (پند + ـش) is 'his advice' or 'advice for him.' دهیم is the corresponding 1st-person-plural subjunctive/imperative 'let us give' (parallel with بکوشیم).
segments.s324-g36: به پند اختر is an instrumental/qualifying phrase: به + پند (by/with the counsel) and اختر (star) specifies whose counsel — 'by the counsel of the star' (astrological or lofty advice). سودمندش is سودمند + ـش 'beneficial to him' (an adjective with a possessive clitic), and دهیم repeats the hortatory verb 'let us give.' The sequence thus prescribes giving him advice that is profitable and guided by the star.
segments.s324-g37: وز آن پس is a compact temporal connective 'and from that after / and then thereafter' (وز here is a poetic contraction of و + ز). هر آن کس که آمد به راه is a relative/subordinate clause: هر آن کس که (whoever) آمد (came) به راه (on the road). The whole segment sets the scene: after that, anyone who arrived on the road ...
segments.s324-g38: برفتند is simple past plural 'they went/departed.' پویان is a participial/adverbial form meaning 'hastening/striving/seeking' and here describes the manner of their leaving (they departed hastily/energetically). سوی بارگاه places the direction 'toward the barġāh' (barġāh = royal court). Together: 'they departed, hastening toward the court.'
segments.s324-g39: هم آنگه is an adverbial temporal phrase 'just then / immediately' (هم as an intensifier + آنگه 'then'). ز در literally 'from the door/gate' (poetic z = از), and پرده برداشتند is a past plural verb phrase 'they lifted/removed the curtain' (برداشتند = they took away/removed). The clause describes the immediate action of uncovering the entrance.
segments.s324-g40: بر اندازه‌شان is a prepositional/measure phrase 'according to their measure/rank' (بر + اندازه + ـشان). شاد is an adjective 'joyful/happy' modifying the ensuing verb, and بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/put' (the b- prefix is the classical verbal prefix). Altogether: 'they joyfully placed (them) according to their rank' (i.e., seated or set people in order).
segments.s324-g65: به introduces the list of accompanying qualities and here functions like English 'with' or 'by' (i.e. 'with/by means of victory, ...'). The nouns are coordinated (پیروزی و مردی و مهر و رای) and list abstract virtues: پیروزی 'victory', مردی 'manliness/bravery', مهر 'kindness/favour', رای 'counsel/wise decision'.
segments.s324-g66: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the wished-for clause. شاهیت is the abstract noun from شاه (here 'kingship' or 'your rule'). بادا is the classical/poetic optative 'may it be'. همیشه 'always' modifies the verb phrase, and به جای literally 'in (its) place' = 'established/standing'.
segments.s324-g67: چه is an exclamative 'how' (expressing admiration). مهتر is a term of high esteem ('distinguished one, great man'). که introduces the following clause describing that state. پای ترا را (here written پای ترا را in older separations) = 'your foot' (پای + second-person suffix + object marker), and خاک نیست literally 'is no dust' i.e. 'dust does not touch it' — a poetic way to say the person (or you) is exalted/unhumbled.
segments.s324-g68: چه again 'how' as an exclamation. زهر here is used figuratively as 'poison'/'harm' (i.e. 'how deadly/terrible'). آنکه = 'he who' (relative pronoun). نام تو 'your name' where نام carries the sense of reputation/fame. تریاک نیست 'is not opium' — opium as a soothing cure; the clause means 'how harmful is he for whom your name is no comfort (no opiate)'.
segments.s324-g69: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an item of news). ناسزا modifies آگهی: 'an abusive/insulting report' (ناسزا = insult, آگهی = information/news). یافتم is the past first-person 'I found/received' (I came upon such news).
segments.s324-g70: بدان is a fused locution (به + آن) meaning 'on/at that' or 'because of that' (i.e. 'upon that news'). آگهی repeats 'news/information'. تیز is an adverbial use 'swift/quick', and بشتافتم is the past 'I hurried' (brought about by that news).
segments.s324-g71: ستاره‌شناسان is a plural compound 'stargazers' (astrologers/astronomers). و is the coordinating 'and'. کنداوران is the plural of کنداور (here 'investigators, searchers, excavators') — a pair of professional types being listed.
segments.s324-g72: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from'. هر کشوری = 'every country/realm' (هر + adjectival form کشوری). آنکه دیدم is a relative clause 'those whom I saw' (آنکه = 'he/that who', دیدم = 'I saw'). سران is the predicate 'were chiefs/foremost men' (here used as a nominative plural predicate identifying the people seen). Note word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'from every country those whom I saw (were) chiefs.'
segments.s324-g17: همه modifies بارگاهش and here means 'the whole/entire' (placed before the noun as is common in Persian). بارگاهش is بارگاه + the possessive suffix -ش 'his'. سیاهست is the older fused form of سیاه + است 'is dark' (poetic/archaic orthography). The final و بس is an elliptic particle meaning 'and that's all' / 'merely' that closes the clause.
segments.s324-g18: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day'. او را places the third‑person pronoun as a definite object (را is the object marker) before the verb. ندیدست is an archaic perfect/past form (ن + دید + ست) meaning 'has not seen / did not see' with کس ('no one') as the (negative) subject following the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s324-g19: از ... تا marks a time span 'from X until Y' (here از این هفته تا آن = 'from this week until that [time]'). این هفته is literal 'this week'. در بارگاه uses در in the sense 'door/gate' (not the locative preposition), so درِ بارگاه = 'the palace gate'.
segments.s324-g20: گشایند is a third‑person plural present (or jussive/optative in older Persian) 'they open'. پوییم is first‑person plural (classical form) meaning 'we go about / search' (the provided lemma senses do not capture this verbal use, so no direct sense matches). یابیم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we find' (here meaning simply 'we find/attain'). راه is the object 'the way/path'; the verbs and object appear in a slightly inverted, poetic order.
segments.s324-g21: جز introduces an exception 'except'. آنست is the contracted form of آن است 'that is'. کیخسرو‌ای combines the proper name کیخسرو with the vocative marker ای ('O'), so the phrase is addressing or identifying Kay Khosrow; پهلوان is a nominative apposition 'champion/hero'. The copula ست is the classical form of است.
segments.s324-g22: که introduces a relative clause 'whom/that'. دیدی is second‑person singular past 'you saw' (classical past form). تو is the emphatic/explicit second‑person pronoun. The adjectives شاداب و روشن‌روان follow the verb and describe the person seen: شاداب 'vigorous/fresh' and روشن‌روان a poetic epithet 'cheerful / bright‑souled'.
segments.s324-g23: شده is a perfect/passive participle 'has become' (from شدن). It takes the adjective کوژ 'bent/curved' as a predicate. بالای سرو is a genitival phrase 'the top/head of the cypress' (بالای = 'above/top of'). سهی is a poetic epithet attached to سرو; here it functions as a descriptive epithet rather than a literal color word (poetic register).
segments.s324-g24: گرفته is the perfect participle of گرفتن used figuratively 'has taken on / assumed' (a color or appearance). گل سرخ رنگ is a compact adjectival phrase 'flower‑red colored' (poetic order: noun + color + رنگ). بهی qualifies the color as pleasant/fair ('good, beautiful'), so the whole phrase describes having acquired a rosy, beautiful red hue.
segments.s324-g81: من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. از درد ایرانیان is an ablative/cause phrase ('because of / from the pain of the Iranians'): از here has an instrumental/causal force. چون عقاب is a simile ('like an eagle') placed after the cause; word order is Subject — cause — simile.
segments.s324-g82: همی is the archaic/adverbial particle indicating continued or emphatic action (here 'continually / did then'); تاختم is the 1st‑person past of تاختن 'I rushed/swooped'. همچو کشتی بر آب is a second simile 'like a ship upon the water' (همچو = 'like', بر = 'on/upon', آب = 'water'). The verb (تاختم) carries the main action; همی gives an epic/habitual flavour.
segments.s324-g83: بدان here functions as a unit meaning 'know this / to that end' (ب‑ + دان; دان is root 'know' used imperatively or as a discourse connective). تا + subjunctive (بپرسم) forms a purpose clause 'so that I may ask'; ز شاه جهان means 'about / from the king of the world' (ز = poetic 'of/from/about').
segments.s324-g84: ز چیزی introduces the object of inquiry 'about something'. که starts a relative clause 'that/which'; دارد is present 'he has/keeps' and with همی (continuous/emphatic particle) expresses ongoing possession or concealment; در نهان = 'in secret / hidden inside'. The relative clause modifies چیزی.
segments.s324-g85: به سه چیز is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'by/through three things'. هر کار نیکو is a noun phrase 'every good deed'; شود is the present/generic verb 'becomes / is accomplished' (classical usage of شود to state general truth). Word order: prepositional phrase — noun phrase — verb.
segments.s324-g86: همان is a demonstrative 'that / thus / the same'. تخت شاهی is a compound 'royal throne' (تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship). بی‌آهو is an adjective 'deerless / without deer' (بی‑ = without + آهو). شود again 'becomes' — the clause states the result: 'that (resulting) the royal throne becomes without deer.'
segments.s324-g87: Repeated به before each noun marks the instrumental/means 'by/with'. گنج = 'treasure', رنج = 'suffering / toil', and مردان مرد is an intensive compound 'men of men' = 'true/heroic men' (mordan repeated for emphasis). The list names the three means mentioned previously.
segments.s324-g88: به جز این = 'except for these / apart from these'. نشاید is an impersonal/archaic negative modal 'it is not fitting / it cannot be' (used to say something is not possible or proper). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here; کار کرد (کار + کرد) = 'to do the work' (کار = the work/deed, کرد = do). Together: 'Except for these, it is not possible to do the work.'
segments.s324-g89: چهارم is an enumerative adverb = “fourth(ly)” introducing this item. به یزدان is a prepositional phrase (به + noun) meaning “to/for God.” ستایش is a noun meaning “praise,” and کنیم is the 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/jussive (“let us do / let us praise”) that makes the whole clause an exhortation.
segments.s324-g90: شب و روز is a fixed adverbial phrase “night and day.” او را is the third-person pronoun with the object marker را (marking the definite object “him/He”). نیایش is a noun “prayer/supplication,” and کنيم (first-person-plural subjunctive) attaches to it to form the exhortation “let us pray (to him).” Word order places the adverbial first, then the object, then the verbal phrase.
segments.s324-g91: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause (“for/that”). اویست here functions as an archaic fused form of the copula (roughly “he is”) in this context. فریادرس is the predicate noun “rescuer/succourer,” and بنده را is the definite object phrase “the servant” marked by را. The clause thus says “for he is the rescuer of the servant.”
segments.s324-g92: هم is an emphatic/adverbial “also/indeed.” او is the subject pronoun “he.” بازدارد is a present-tense (classical) form meaning “keeps back / restrains.” گراینده را is the object (گراینده “one who causes to incline / who misleads”) with را marking it. Word order is subject–verb–object in this clause.
segments.s324-g93: به درویش is an indirect object phrase “to the dervish/poor man.” بخشیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/jussive “let us give/bestow.” بسیار چیز is a noun phrase “many things,” with بسیار functioning as a quantitative modifier before چیز. The clause is an exhortation to give to the dervish.
segments.s324-g94: اگرچند is a concessive conjunction “although / even if.” چیز ارجمند است is a simple copular clause “a thing is valuable,” with ارجمند as the predicate adjective and است the copula. نیز at the end adds “also/too” or a sense of “even so.” Overall: “even if the thing is valuable.”
segments.s324-g95: بدان is the imperative “know this,” here formed from the prefix ب- + دان (دان = “know”). تا introduces a purpose/result clause “so that/in order that.” روان تو روشن کند is a subordinate clause in which روان تو (“your soul/spirit”) is the object and روشن کند (third-person singular subjunctive/potential) means “may make/enlighten.” The overall sense: “Know this so that it may enlighten your soul.”
segments.s324-g96: خرد is the subject “wisdom.” پیش مغز تو is a prepositional/location phrase literally “before your brain/mind” (پیش = before/in front of + مغز = brain; تو = your). جوشن کند is the verb phrase “makes (into) a cuirass/puts on a cuirass,” where جوشن (“cuirass/armor”) is used metaphorically as protection. The clause says wisdom should put a protective cuirass before your mind.
segments.s324-g25: ندانم is a poetic negative present: ن + دانم = "I do not know" (equivalent to modern نمی‌دانم). چه introduces the interrogative object "what/which" (here "what kind of"). چشمِ بد is a compound meaning "evil/bad gaze" (چشم = gaze/look, بد = bad), آمد is used intransitively as "befell/happened (came) upon", and بر اوی is the older prepositional phrase "upon him/her" (اوی = archaic pronoun). Word order is literal: "I do not know what bad gaze came upon him."
segments.s324-g26: چرا is the interrogative "why." پژمرید is a third‑person resultative/past form meaning "withered/has withered." آن is the demonstrative "that," چو is the simile "like/as," گلبرگ is "petal," and روی here means "face/cheek" — so the clause literally asks "why did that face, like a petal, wither?" The simile چو modifies روی.
segments.s324-g27: مگر here is rhetorical/pregnant ("could it be that / perhaps"). تیره شد is a verbal predicate: تیره "ominous/dark" + شد "became" (past of شدن) and بخت ایرانیان is the subject phrase "the fortune/fate of the Iranians." Literally: "Could it be that the Iranians' fate became dark?"
segments.s324-g28: وگر begins a conditional/rhetorical alternative "or if / but if." شاه را uses the objective/topic marker را after "king," ز اختر "from the star(s)" (poetic for astrological influence), آمد here means "befell/arrived (happened)", and زیان is "harm/detriment." The clause asks whether misfortune came to the king because of the stars.
segments.s324-g29: بدیشان is a dative/prepositional adverb meaning "to them." چنین is an adverb "thus/so." گفت is the past verbal predicate "said" (here used with the subject that follows); زال دلیر is the nominative subject "Zal the brave." Note the poetic word order: indirect object + adverb + verb + subject.
segments.s324-g30: که باشد که is a formulaic double־که introducing a tentative/rhetorical question ("what/could it be that..."). باشد is the subjunctive/potential form of بودن here meaning "might be / could be." شاه آمد is a simple past clause "the king came/befell," and از گاه سیر is an ablative phrase "from a time of satedness/satiety" (سیر = satiety) — i.e. "has the king come from a time of being satiated/fulfilled?"
segments.s324-g31: درستی here means "uprightness/honesty," و هم is the coordinating "and also," دردمندی is a noun meaning "suffering/affliction," and بود is the past copula "there was/was." The phrase states two coexisting conditions: honesty and suffering.
segments.s324-g32: گهی ... گه is a pair meaning "now and then / alternately." گاهی خوشی = "sometimes happiness/joy," و گه نژندی = "and at times baseness/disgrace," and بود is again the past copula "was." The line contrasts alternating fortunes.
segments.s324-g41: چو here is the comparative/simile marker 'as/like' introducing a list of figures. دستان is used as a proper name in the poem (not the common noun senses given), then و links items, چو repeats the simile before the next name, and پیلتن is a place‑name attached to رستم as a specifier. The phrase is a terse poetic listing: 'like Dastān and like Rostam of Piltan.'
segments.s324-g42: Another short series introduced by چو 'as/like' — طوس here refers to the hero Tous (not the city). The repeated و simply connects names; آن انجمن is 'that assembly/meeting', with آن demonstrative pointing back to the group just named.
segments.s324-g43: Again a list begun by چو 'like': گرگین and بیژن are proper names (Gorgin, Bijan). گستهم here is a personal name (Gostaham) rather than the verbal sense listed, so the segment names several heroes in parallel.
segments.s324-g44: هر آن کس که is a relative clause: 'every/each person who…'. رفتند is a past plural verb ('they went' or here a poetic plural used with generic/collective reference) and گردان is the noun 'troop/host' that the clause relates to; به هم means 'together' or 'into one'. The clause describes anyone who came/assembled the troops together.
segments.s324-g45: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor'. چو in this clause functions temporally ('when' / 'since' in classical usage) introducing the subordinate clause 'when he saw'. روی ایشان بدید = 'he saw their faces' — بدید is the simple past 'saw'.
segments.s324-g46: The verb شنید 'he heard' comes at the end as typical in Persian. به here gives a locative/directional nuance ('at/toward'), پرده‌در is an epithet literally 'curtain‑opener' (the person or place at the curtain), and آوای رستم is the genitive 'the voice of Rostam' — so 'he heard Rostam's voice at the curtain.'
segments.s324-g47: پراندیشه is an adjectival/participial phrase 'full of thought / pensive' modifying the king. از تخت 'from the throne' + بر پای خاست 'rose to his feet' uses بر + noun to indicate movement toward/onto the feet; خاست is the simple past 'he rose.'
segments.s324-g48: چنان 'thus/so' introduces the result: پشت خمیده را (the bent back) is marked with را as a definite object, and کرد راست = 'made straight' (کرد 'caused/made' + راست 'straight'). The object precedes the verb in this poetic order and راست functions as the resulting state.
segments.s324-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among' placed before the following noun phrase. دانندگان is a plural agent noun 'the knowers / learned men' and is the noun governed by ز. هرکه is an indefinite relative 'whoever' selecting individuals from that group. بد here is an archaic/poetic reduced form (see note) functioning as a past copula 'was' (classical contraction of بود) rather than the adjective 'bad' — it links هرکه to the following nisba (زابلی). زابلی is a nisba/adjective meaning 'a person from Zabul'.
segments.s324-g50: ز again = 'from' introducing a list of places/origins. قنوج is a place name (Kannauj) used as origin. وز here is actually the fusion of و + ز ('and from'), common in poetry; in the edition it appears as one token but functions as 'and from'. دنبر is another place/name in the list (treated as another origin). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the last item. کابلی is a nisba 'a Kabuli / from Kabul'.
segments.s324-g51: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'. بپرسید is a past narrative form meaning 'he questioned / he asked' (the prefixed ب is the classical narrative marker). و is the conjunction 'and'. بنواختشان is the verb نواختن with the narrative prefix ب- and the object suffix -شان 'them'; it means 'he made them play / had music played for them' (to cause music/sound directed at them).
segments.s324-g52: به is a preposition 'in/according to' or 'to/for' governing the ensuing nominal phrase. رسم here means 'custom / ceremonial manner'. مهی is an adjectival epithet 'moon‑like / beautiful', qualifying رسم (a poetic epithet for a splendid ceremony). پایگه is 'station / seat / establishment' (what is being set up for them). ساختشان is the past verb ساختن with the object suffix -شان 'he made / he set up for them'.
segments.s324-g53: همان نیز = 'the same likewise' or 'similarly'. ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the class Iranian. ایرانیان is the plural 'Iranians'. هرکه is 'whoever', and بود is the past copula 'was' (he/there was) completing the relative clause 'whoever there was among the Iranians.'
segments.s324-g54: به here introduces a measure/standard: 'according to / to'. اندازه‌شان is a possessive compound 'their measure / their rank/degree' (the standard by which something is done). پایگه is 'station/position' (the thing conferred). برفزود is a classical past form meaning 'he raised / he bestowed' — here 'he raised/bestowed a station' for them.
segments.s324-g55: بر is the preposition 'upon / to / concerning' marking the object of praise. او is the third‑person pronoun 'him'. آفرین here is the interjection/noun 'bravo / praise' used as the content of praise. کرد is the past auxiliary/verb 'did / made' so آفرین کرد = 'he praised'. بسیار is an adverb 'greatly / much'. زال is the agent 'Zal' (the subject performing the praising). Word order is typical Persian: object phrase + verb + manner + subject.
segments.s324-g56: که introduces the content clause / direct speech 'that' or 'saying that'. شادان is an adjectival predicate 'joyful / merry' used here as a wish. بدی in this poetic text is not the adjective 'bad' but an archaic subjunctive/present form of بودن ('be') or contraction thereof (functioning 'may you be'); the dictionary senses given do not include this verbal use, so its lexical list does not match (see sense index). تا is the temporal conjunction 'as long as / until'. بود is the pastcopula 'was / exists' used here with ماه و سال 'month and year', so the clause expresses a wish 'may you be joyful as long as months and years exist.'
segments.s324-g57: ز گاه X تا Y is a poetic time-phrase: ز (archaic/poetic variant of از) + گاه 'time/era' followed by the two names connected by تا 'until'. Word order is 'from the time of Manuchehr to Kay Qobad' (prepositional phrase marking a span).
segments.s324-g58: از آن نامداران که داریم یاد is a noun phrase with a relative clause: از آن نامداران 'of/from those renowned ones' and که داریم یاد 'whom we remember' (که introduces the relative clause). داریم + یاد literally 'we have remembrance' = 'we remember'.
segments.s324-g59: همان زو ... کی is an emphatic nominative: همان 'the same/those very' + ز (poetic 'from/among') introducing the examples; the names follow, and کی is a relative/pronoun 'who' linking to the following description. The sequence lists persons singled out from the preceding group.
segments.s324-g60: بزرگان و شاهان فرخنده‌پی is a coordinated noun phrase: 'great ones and kings' with the postposed epithet فرخنده‌پی ('fortunate-footed' / felicitous epithet) modifying شاهان. In Persian classical style adjectives/epithets often follow the noun.
segments.s324-g61: سیاوش مرا خود چو فرزند بود has the object/benefactive phrase 'مرا' ('to me' / 'for me') before the verb (classical word order), with خود emphatic 'himself' and چو فرزند 'as/like a son' forming a simile; بود is the past copula 'was'. Note the object-marking را after the clitic (here split in the token list).
segments.s324-g62: که با فر و با برز و اورند بود is a relative clause introduced by که 'for/which', with repeated با 'with' marking a list of qualities possessed by the subject: فر (the divine royal glory, farr), برز (exaltation/highness), and اورند (here a proper name/attribute). بود is again the past 'was' indicating possession ('he was with/possessed').
segments.s324-g63: ندیدم کسی را بدین بخردی: ندیدم is negative past 'I did not see'; followed by the object کسی را 'anyone' (را marks the definite/object), then بدین بخردی 'with this wisdom' where بدین = به + این (archaic/poetic 'with/by this') and بخردی is an abstract noun 'wisdom/prudence'. The clause states the speaker's negative assessment.
segments.s324-g64: بدین برز و این فره ایزدی continues the list of qualities: بدین (with this) + برز (exaltation) and این فره ایزدی 'this divine farr' (فره 'farr', ایزدی 'divine' as a postpositive adjective). The parallel demonstratives (بدین / این) emphasize the exceptional nature of those attributes.
segments.s324-g73: The preposition ز at the start ('from') governs a list of origins; the list items are joined by و (and). In classical/poetic Persian repeated ز is often elided or written fused with و (here written as وز), so the segment names a series of places from which they came. Word order is preposition + noun, then coordinating particles linking the following items.
segments.s324-g74: برفتند is a 3rd-person plural past (they went/set out); the prefix بر- gives a classical/poetic sense of 'go forth.' با introduces the accompanying instrument or means (with an Indian zij). ز جای is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the place' marking origin or departure point.
segments.s324-g75: بدان here functions as 'with that / thereby' (classical contraction of با + آن) linking back to the previous clause; تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that.' بجویند is present-subjunctive (3rd person plural) meaning 'may seek' or 'would seek' and takes راز سپهر ('the secret/mystery of the heavens') as its object.
segments.s324-g76: کز is the fused relative/prepositional form (that/from which); it introduces a relative clause modifying راز سپهر. ایران is the noun governed by the preposition meaning 'from Iran.' چرا is the interrogative 'why' inside the indirect question; پاک is an adverb/adjective 'pure/entirely'; ببرید is a past-tense form 'took/removed' (they carried away); مهر here refers to the celestial entity/favor (classical usage tied to the sun/Mithra), i.e. what was taken away.
segments.s324-g77: از ایران کس آمد is a basic clause 'someone came from Iran' (کس = somebody, آمد = came). The conjunction/relative که links this to the following reporting clause: the person who came is the one of whom the king speaks/commands. پیروز شاه is a noun phrase 'the victorious king' (adjective + noun) and is the subject of the following verb.
segments.s324-g78: بفرمود is the polite/honorific past of گفتن/فرمودن 'he commanded/ordered/said.' تا here begins the object/purpose clause introduced by the command ('that ...'); پرده بارگاه is the direct object of that subordinate clause (the 'curtain of the palace'). The verb of that subordinate clause follows in the next sentence.
segments.s324-g79: نه بردارد is a negative jussive/subjunctive construction: نه + 3rd-person singular verb meaning 'let him not lift' or 'he shall not lift.' از پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before' and سالار is its object 'the commander/chief.' بار here is the noun that refers back to the curtain/covering (the text uses synoptic vocabulary); the whole phrase gives the location/condition under which lifting is forbidden.
segments.s324-g80: بپوشد is 3rd-person singular subjunctive/jussive of پوشیدن 'to cover/conceal' and completes the ordered subordinate clause introduced by بفرمود تا: 'that he cover/conceal.' ز ما is a prepositional phrase 'from us' (elliptical: 'from our sight'), and چهره شهریار is the object 'the face of the king' (possessive noun phrase). Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and object in this poetic style.

sentences.s324-s5: That Tus, with the Kayanian standard, and all the famed men in gilded shoes.
sentences.s324-s6: When Goudarz came before Tahmatan, a tear-drop from his eyelashes fell upon his face.
sentences.s324-s7: The army marched on; faces were pale for the king, and every heart was full of burning grief and pain.
sentences.s324-s8: They told Zāl and Rostam that the king, by the counsel of Iblis, had lost his way.
sentences.s324-s1: At the beginning of the week Zal and Rostam met each other, their hearts unfulfilled and full of sorrow.
sentences.s324-s2: When the Iranians learned the news, everyone, hearts burning, hurried forward.
sentences.s324-s3: When Rostam appeared, and Zal clad in gold, those same mōbeds displayed abundant skill.
sentences.s324-s4: Every man who belonged to the golden stock had a steed prepared for his reception.
sentences.s324-s17: You often turn your hearts toward grief, so that from sorrow the soul becomes at once joyful and sullen.
sentences.s324-s18: Let us strive and give him much counsel; let us give him, by the star’s counsel, profitable advice.
sentences.s324-s19: And thereafter whoever came upon the road, the seekers departed toward the royal court.
sentences.s324-s20: Right then they lifted the curtain from the doorway and, happily and according to their rank, set them down.
sentences.s324-s33: May your kingship always stand firm with victory, manliness, kindness, and wise counsel.
sentences.s324-s34: How exalted! — that dust does not lie on your feet; how poisonous is he for whom your name is not an opiate.
sentences.s324-s35: I received a certain insulting report; at that news I hastened quickly.
sentences.s324-s36: The astrologers and investigators — from every country, those I saw were chiefs/leading men.
sentences.s324-s9: His entire palace is dark, and that's all; night and day no one has seen him.
sentences.s324-s10: From this week until that time they open the palace gate, and we search and find the way.
sentences.s324-s11: Except that — Kay Khosrow, O champion — whom you saw fresh and of cheerful spirit.
sentences.s324-s12: The top of the lofty cypress has become bent; it has taken on a rosy‑red hue like a flower.
sentences.s324-s41: Because of the Iranians' suffering I rushed like an eagle, like a ship upon the water.
sentences.s324-s42: To that end — so that I may ask the king of the world about something he keeps hidden.
sentences.s324-s43: By three things every good deed is accomplished; thus the royal throne becomes without deer.
sentences.s324-s44: By treasure, by hardship, and by heroic men — apart from these, this work cannot be done.
sentences.s324-s45: Fourth: let us praise God; let us pray to Him night and day.
sentences.s324-s46: For he is the rescuer of the servant; he also restrains the one who leads astray.
sentences.s324-s47: Let us give many things to the dervish, even if some things are valuable.
sentences.s324-s48: Know this so that it may enlighten your soul; let wisdom set a cuirass before your mind.
sentences.s324-s13: I do not know what ill eye fell upon him; why did that face, like a rose‑petal, wither?
sentences.s324-s14: Could it be that the Iranians' fortune has turned dark, or did harm come to the king from the stars?
sentences.s324-s15: Zal the brave said this to them: "What can it be — has the king come from a time of satiety?"
sentences.s324-s16: There was uprightness and also suffering; sometimes joy and sometimes disgrace.
sentences.s324-s21: As Dastān and as Rostam of Piltan, as Tous and as Goudarz, and that assembly.
sentences.s324-s22: Like Gorgin and like Bijan and Gostaham — every man who had come together with the troop.
sentences.s324-s23: When the king saw their faces, he heard the voice of Rostam at the curtain.
sentences.s324-s24: Pensive, he rose from the throne to his feet; thus he made straight the bent back.
sentences.s324-s25: Of the learned men — whoever was a Zābeli, from Kannauj, from Dunbar, or a Kabuli.
sentences.s324-s26: He questioned them one by one and had them play; he established for them a station in the moon‑like (splendid) manner.
sentences.s324-s27: Likewise, of the Iranians whoever there was, he raised for them a station proportionate to their rank.
sentences.s324-s28: Zal praised him greatly, saying (wishing) that he be joyful as long as month and year endure.
sentences.s324-s29: From the time of Manuchehr to Kay Qobad — of those famed men whose memory we keep.
sentences.s324-s30: Those were Tahmasp and Kavus — great and fortunate-footed kings.
sentences.s324-s31: Siyavash was, to me, like a son; for he possessed farr, barz, and Orand.
sentences.s324-s32: I have seen no one (lit. 'I did not see anyone') of such wisdom, of such exaltation, and of this divine farr.
sentences.s324-s37: From Kannauj, and from Denvar and Margh and Mai, they set out with an Indian zij from the place.
sentences.s324-s38: With it, so that they might seek the secret of the heavens — why the sun had taken purity from Iran.
sentences.s324-s39: Someone came from Iran, whom the victorious king commanded (to act regarding) the palace curtain.
sentences.s324-s40: He ordered that no one lift it before the commander, and that the king’s face be covered from our sight.
